summary: Spolsky argues that patent trolls operate a protection racket indistinguishable from organized crime. They buy vague software patents, send threatening letters to small developers, and profit when victims pay up rather than face million-dollar legal costs. He condemns the practice of paying trolls as complicit and calls on developers to band together through organizations like the EFF, Stack Exchange's Ask Patents, and the Application Developers Alliance to fight back and reform the patent system.
key_points: Patent troll lawsuits have more than tripled since 1999, making it one of the fastest-growing industries in the US
The troll business model exploits the asymmetry between cheap threat letters and million-dollar legal defense costs
Paying patent trolls is morally wrong because it funds their next round of attacks on other developers
Collective action through organizations like the EFF and Ask Patents is the civilized response to what amounts to organized crime
money_quotes: The recipients of the letter need to think that it's a threat to sue if they don't pay up, but in court, the letter has to look like an invitation to license some exciting new technology. In other words it has to be just on this side of extortion.
It is organized crime, plain and simple. It is an abuse of the legal system, an abuse of the patent system, and a moral affront.
In the face of organized crime, civilized people don't pay up.
When you pay up, you're funding the criminals, which makes you complicit in their next attacks.
If you pay the trolls, giving them money and comfort to go after the next round of indie developers, you're not just being 'pragmatic,' you have actually gone over to the dark side.
Life is a bit hard sometimes, and sometimes you have to step up and fight fights that you never signed up for.
Civilized people don't pay up. They band together, and fight, and eliminate the problem.
key_insight: Patent trolling is a protection racket that thrives because individual developers rationally choose to pay rather than fight, making collective resistance the only viable solution.
